coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots via ion modification for improving photoluminescence stability in aqueous solution with heavy metal ions
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Juan
Li, Pei
Song, Zailing
Li, Jie
Yang, Huan
Yan, Fan
Li, Lu
Xu, Can
Chen, Junlang
Chen, Liang
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have shown growing potential in the applications of ion detection, anti-counterfeiting ink and light-emitting devices. However, the photoluminescence (PL) properties of GQDs will be lost when they interact with some heavy metal ions. Effective strategies to improve PL stability are still limited. In this work, we prepared a new kind of GQDs modified by scandium ions (Sc3+) to improve the PL of GQDs in aqueous solution containing heavy metal ions. The Sc3+ modified GQDs (Sc@GQDs) show a slight fluorescence enhancement compared with pristine GQDs. In addition, the PL of pristine GQDs could be quenched by Fe3+/Cu2+/Cr3+, while the PL of Sc@GQDs was kept stable with these heavy metal ions. Meanwhile, GQDs modified by Al3+ and Ga3+ exhibit similar results, which further verifies the universality of this method that GQDs modified by specific metal ions to maintain the PL of GQDs with heavy metal ions. As applications, Sc@GQDs may have great potential in the fields of fluorescent flexible device and fluorescent ink.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Ion modification
Photoluminescence stability
Fluorescentmaterials

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence intensity of GQDs and Sc@GQDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (12074341, U1832150, 11875236), the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (No. LQ20B030002), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Provincial Universities of Zhejiang (2020TD001). The authors thank Zhongteng Wang and Prof. Jing Xu for their helpful discussion.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) AFM image of GQDs. (b) TEM image of GQDs. (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of GQDs and Sc@GQDs. (d) FT-IR spectra of GQDs and Sc@GQDs. (e) XPS survey spectra of GQDs and Sc@GQDs. (f) Time resolved fluorescence spectra of GQDs and Sc@GQDs.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of GQDs at different excitation wavelengths from 310 nm to 370 nm. The inset highlights the GQDs solution under natural and UV lights. (b) The relative fluorescence ratios of GQDs and Sc@GQDs with and without Fe3+, Cu2+ and Cr3+ ions (F0 and F represent the PL intensity before and after adding heavy metal ions). (c) The relative fluorescence ratios of Sc@GQDs with Fe3+ at different Sc3+ concentrations.
(a) PL intensity of GQDs, Sc@GQDs, Ga@GQDs and Al@GQDs. (b) PL intensity of Sc@GQDs + Fe3+, Ga@GQDs + Fe3+, Al@GQDs + Fe3+ and GQDs + Fe3+. (c) is the relative fluorescence ratio of (b).
(a) C1s XPS spectra of Sc@GQDs, (b) GQDs + Fe3+ and (c) Sc@GQDs + Fe3+.

(a) Image of GQDs (up left), GQDs + Fe3+(down left), Sc@GQDs (up right) and Sc@GQDs + Fe3+ (down right) under natural light, (b-d) are the images of four kinds of powder under 365 nm UV light at day 1, day 10 and day 40, respectively.

PVA film (left) and Sc@GQDs/PVA film (right) under (a) natural light and (b) 365 nm UV light. The fluorescence of bent PVA film (left) and Sc@GQDS/PVA film (right) under (c) natural light and (d) 365 nm UV light. (e, f) Writing trace under natural light and 365 nm UV light. (g, h) Painting trace under natural light and 365 nm UV light.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots via ion modification for improving photoluminescence stability in aqueous solution with heavy metal ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Optical Engineering, College of Optical, Mechanical and Electrical Engineering, Zhejiang A&F University, Hangzhou 311300, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Optical, Mechanical and Electrical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Optical Engineering, College of Optical, Mechanical and Electrical Engineering, Zhejiang A&F University, Hangzhou 311300, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physical Science and Technology, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physical Science and Technology, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Lab for Magnetism and Magnetic Materials of MOE

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Lab for Magnetism and Magnetic Materials of MOE, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 73000, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have shown growing potential in the applications of ion detection, anti-counterfeiting ink and light-emitting devices. However, the photoluminescence (PL) properties of GQDs will be lost when they interact with some heavy metal ions. Effective strategies to improve PL stability are still limited. In this work, we prepared a new kind of GQDs modified by scandium ions (Sc3+) to improve the PL of GQDs in aqueous solution containing heavy metal ions. The Sc3+ modified GQDs (Sc@GQDs) show a slight fluorescence enhancement compared with pristine GQDs. In addition, the PL of pristine GQDs could be quenched by Fe3+/Cu2+/Cr3+, while the PL of Sc@GQDs was kept stable with these heavy metal ions. Meanwhile, GQDs modified by Al3+ and Ga3+ exhibit similar results, which further verifies the universality of this method that GQDs modified by specific metal ions to maintain the PL of GQDs with heavy metal ions. As applications, Sc@GQDs may have great potential in the fields of fluorescent flexible device and fluorescent ink.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), composed of sp2-carbon domain structures, defects and functional groups, are novel fluorescent zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials [1–3]. Owing to their tunable photoluminescence (PL), good photo-stability, water solubility, low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility [4–6], GQDs have attracted growing interest in the research society. These outstanding features endow them with great potential in various applications, such as cellular imaging, energy storage, metal ion detection and anti-counterfeiting ink [7–10]. Generally, the PL mechanism can be mainly divided into two categories: one is the quantum confinement effect of conjugated π-domains [11–13], and the other is the surface/edge states in GQDs [14].

The existence of heavy metal ions (such as Fe3+ [15–17], Cu2+ [10,17,18], Ag+ [17], Hg2+ [18,19], Cr3+ [10,20], Ru3+ [20], Co2+ [20,21] and Ni2+ [20,22]) will lead to the PL quenching of GQDs. There are two possible mechanisms corresponding to this PL quenching. On the one hand, the cation-π interactions between GQDs and some heavy metal ions will destroy the π-π bonds, and then quench the PL of GQDs. The cation-π interaction is the electrostatic attraction between the cation and the π electron system [23–25] and it is regarded as an important non-covalent force. Li et al. reported that Fe3+, Cu2+ and Cr3+ were adsorbed on the center of aromatic rings of GQDs, resulting in the reduction of the π-conjugated regions and PL quenching [10]. On the other hand, the PL of sulfur doped GQDs (S-GQDs) can be quenched by the adsorption of Fe3+ ions on the edge of phenolic hydroxyl groups [15]. Then, the electrons in the excited state of S-GQDs will be transferred to the 3d orbit of Fe3+ ions, thus promoting the composite annihilation of non-radiative electrons/holes [16]. Additionally, the hydroxyl groups on GQDs co-doped with nitrogen and sulfur (N, S-GQDs) are able to coordinate with Fe3+ ions to form metal hydroxides [17]. While for Cu2+ ions, they are able to interact with the electron-donating N-dopants on GQDs, leading to PL quenching [17,18]. Obviously, the PL of GQDs can be quenched by many kinds of heavy metal ions, which is harmful to the PL stability of GQDs and the various applications of GQDs.

Currently, researchers attempt to restore the PL of GQDs quenched by heavy metal ions through two strategies. One is that the affinity between metal ions and chelating agents is stronger than that between GQDs and metal ions. Therefore, chelating agents (such as cysteine [17,26,27] and ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) [17]) can restore the PL after metal ions are taken away from GQDs’ surface by chelating agents. The other is ionic control strategy. For example, As3+ ions adsorbed on the negatively charged surface of Fe-GQDs, neutralize the charge on the surface of Fe-GQDs to achieve PL enhancement [28]. The PL of GQDs can also be quenched by Cu2+ ions through the adsorption of Cu2+ on the center of π-conjugated regions surrounded by hydroxyl groups. However, Al3+ ions can restore the π-conjugated region both in unoxidized and oxidized regions of Cu2+@GQDs [10], leading to the recovery of PL. Moreover, the binding of Al3+ and GQDs restrict the intramolecular rotation, prevent the non-radiative decay path, and enhance the PL of GQDs [29]. In addition, Hg2+ ions can significantly quench the PL intensity of GQDs. When CN– ions are added into Hg2+@GQDs solution, Hg2+@GQDs interact with CN– to form Hg(CN)2, which restore the PL of GQDs [19]. In spite of the previous efforts to restore the PL of GQDs quenched by heavy metal ions, little is known about how to maintain the PL of GQDs without quenching.

In this work, we prepared two kinds of GQDs with and without Sc3+ modification, using starch as precursor. Sc3+ modified GQDs (Sc@GQDs) show better PL stability than pristine GQDs in heavy metal solution (containing Fe3+, Cu2+ or Cr3+). We further explored the potential applications of Sc@GQDs in the fluorescent flexible device and fluorescent ink. In addition, AFM images, UV–vis and FT-IR spectra were adopted to characterize the morphology of GQDs and the surface functional groups.

All chemical reagents, namely, starch, FeCl3, CuCl2, CrCl3·6H2O, ScCl3·6H2O, AlCl3·6H2O, GaCl3 and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (China). All chemicals in our work were of analytical grade and could be used without further processing. Aqueous chloride solution of various salts was prepared from deionized water with an electrical resistivity of 18.2 MΩ‧cm.

GQDs were prepared by a green and effective one-pot hydrothermal method. In detail, 0.24 g starch powder was added into 100 mL deionized water and the mixture was stirred for 15 min at 333 K in a magnetic stirrer. Next, the uniform solution was further heated at 463 K for 2 h in an autoclave. Then, a pale-yellow suspension was obtained and the suspension was centrifuged at 15000 rpm for 20 min to remove brown impurities. The concentration of the as-prepared GQDs suspension was approximate 2.4 mg/mL, and the reaction yield was approximate 11.67%. Then, 0.26 g ScCl3·6H2O was added into 10 mL GQDs suspension and the mixture was sonicated for 2 h to obtain Sc@GQDs.

Spectra of GQDs under ultraviolet (UV) light (wavelength 365 nm) were detected by a UV analyzer (ZF-7N, Shanghai Jiapeng Technology Co., Ltd.). All fluorescence spectra were obtained by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-4600, Hong Kong Tian mei Co., Ltd.), with all slit widths of 0.5 nm. The concentration of GQDs solution was 2.4 mg/mL. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were performed via a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha spectrometer. Excitation source was Al Kα ray (hv = 1486.60 eV), and the charge correction was carried out according to the energy standard of C1s at 284.70 eV. Avantage 5.52 software was used to analyze XPS data. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured using a Nicolet iS50 spectrograph. The spectral range of FT-IR was 400–4000 cm−1, and the spectral resolution was 4 cm−1. Freeze-drying was performed by a gland vacuum freeze dryer (LGJ-10, Beijing Songyuan Huaxing Co., Ltd.) to evaporate water to prepare GQDs powder for XPS and FT-IR measurements. Atomic force microscopic (AFM, NanoScope V, Bruker) images were obtained under the type of multimode-8 using a silicon tip coated with gold with a force constant of 3.10–37.60 N/m. The substrate was mica. The concentration of GQDs solution for AFM measurement was 0.02 mg/mL. Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images were obtained by JEM-2100F microscope. The energy spectrum model was Oxford Xmax-80 T. The point resolution was 0.25 nm and the line resolution was 0.102 nm. The concentration of GQDs solution for TEM measurement was 0.8 mg/mL.

The morphology of GQDs was studied by AFM. We observe that GQDs are well dispersed, and the thickness of most GQDs is approximate 0.6 nm (see Fig. 1
a). This thickness indicates that GQDs are monolayer [30]. TEM image shows that GQDs have a particle size distribution of 0.5–2.5 nm (see Fig. 1 b), and the size range of the highest percentage is about 1.0–2.0 nm, and the lattice space is 0.22 nm (Fig. 1 b inset). Fig. 1 c shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of pristine GQDs and Sc@GQDs. The absorption peak at 228 nm is designated to the π-π* transition [31–33]. Additionally, the characteristic band of polyaromatic structure appears at 282 nm, which belongs to the n-π transition [32–34]. The two curves are almost overlapped, indicating that Sc3+ ions have no influence on the π-π structures of GQDs at aqueous phase. However, when GQD solution was freeze-dried into powder, Sc3+ ions will affect the π-π stacking of GQDs in powder state. As shown in Fig. 1 d, the peaks at 3120 cm−1 and 1610 cm−1 of Sc@GQDs are much higher than those of GQDs. These peaks are designated to the stretching vibration of O-H, C-H and C=C bonds of carbon rings. Therefore, the higher peaks indicate less π-π stacking structures in GQD powder. That is, the presence of Sc3+ is not beneficial to π-π stacking because of steric effects and the interactions between Sc3+ and GQDs. On the other hand, the peak at 1014 cm−1 (which is assigned to the O-C-O bonds) of Sc@GQDs is weaker than that of pristine GQDs, indicating that interactions between Sc3+ and GQDs mainly occur on the oxygen-containing groups.

XPS spectra of pristine GQDs and Sc@GQDs are presented in Fig. 1 e. GQDs mainly consist of two elements C1s at 284.70 eV and O1s at 532.30 eV, with the atomic percentage of C-58.76%, O-41.24%. As comparison, Sc and chlorine (Cl) are detected in Sc@GQDs, except for C and O. The peaks at 402.70 eV and 198.50 eV correspond to Sc2p and Cl2p. The atomic percentages of C, O, Sc and Cl in Sc@GQDs are 61.69%, 21.89%, 3.82% and 12.60%, respectively. The fluorescence lifetime (Fig. 1 f) of pristine GQDs is 1.16 ns, close to that of Sc@GQDs (1.20 ns), implying that Sc3+ ions are not bound to the PL regions of GQDs, which is consistent with the FT-IR spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence intensity of GQDs and Sc@GQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
As shown in the inset of Fig. 2
a, GQDs exhibit good dispersibility in water. And they are pale yellow and light blue under natural and 365 nm UV lights. We further tested the fluorescence spectra of GQDs under excitation wavelength from 310 nm to 370 nm (Fig. 2 a). The emission peaks gradually red shift from 400 nm to 430 nm with the increasing excitation wavelength, indicating that the fluorescence of GQDs is dependent on the excitation wavelength, and such excitation-dependent behavior can be explained by a wide distribution of particle size of GQDs [35,36]. When the excitation wavelength is 340 nm, the maximum emission peak is localized at 430 nm. Therefore, 340 nm is selected as the optimal excitation wavelength in the following PL quenching and PL maintenance experiments.

It has been reported that the PL of GQDs is sensitive to metal cations, and heavy metal cations can selectively quench fluorescence, such as Fe3+ [15–17], Cu2+ [17,18] Cr3+ [10] and Ag+ [17]. We therefore chose three typical heavy metal ions (Fe3+, Cu2+ and Cr3+) for PL quenching experiments. Chloride solution of Fe3+, Cu2+ and Cr3+ was added to GQDs and Sc@GQDs, and the concentrations of Fe3+, Cu2+ and Cr3+ in the two kind of mixtures are 0.30 mM, 60 mM and 30 mM, respectively. Next, each solution is dispersed by sonication and then analyzed by fluorescence spectrometer. Fig. 2 b shows that the fluorescence of GQDs with Fe3+, Cu2+ or Cr3+ is quenched significantly, as the corresponding fluorescence radios decrease to 0.36, 0.33, and 0.38, compared with the PL intensity of pristine GQDs solution. This is consistent with early reports, and the PL quenching is ascribed to the cation-π interactions between Fe3+, Cu2+ or Cr3+ and aromatic rings on GQDs. Interestingly, when they are put into the Sc@GQDs solution, the PL quenching effect is greatly weakened. The relative fluorescence ratios of Sc@GQDs with Fe3+, Cu2+ and Cr3+ are 0.86, 0.73 and 0.68, respectively. That is, Sc@GQDs exhibit an excellent PL stability in the heavy metal ion solution. We therefore can speculate that the binding sites of Sc3+ are not on the center of aromatic rings but around the defects or oxygen-containing groups on GQDs, which is in good agreement with UV-vis and FT-IR spectra (Fig. 1 b, c). As a result, Sc3+ at these sites will not weaken the PL intensity of GQDs. On the other hand, Sc3+ can prevent heavy metal ions being adsorbed on GQDs’ surface, therefore, the PL of Sc@GQDs will not be quenched by heavy metal ions.

To investigate the concentration of Sc3+ on PL stability of GQDs with heavy metal ions, we measured the relative PL intensity of GQDs with both Fe3+ and Sc3+ at various concentration of Sc3+ (Fig. 2 c). The PL intensity is enhanced directly with the increasing Sc3+ concentration, as the relative fluorescence ratios are 0.36, 0.53, 0.69, 0.80, and 0.86 with the Sc3+ concentrations varied from 0 to 100 mM. Early quantum chemistry calculation reports that Fe3+, Cu2+ and Cr3+ prefer to be adsorbed on the center of aromatic rings. Therefore, with the increasing Sc3+ concentration, more and more Sc3+ ions reach GQDs’ surface, leading to the desorption of Fe3+ and PL maintenance.

We further tested the PL stability of GQDs modified by non-transition metal ions in the presence of Fe3+, using high-valence Ga3+ and Al3+ ions as examples. As shown in Fig. 3 , the PL intensity of GQDs with Sc3+, Ga3+ and Al3+ is slightly stronger than that of pristine GQDs (Fig. 3 a). Similarly, compared with that GQDs + Fe3+, the PL intensities of Sc@GQDs + Fe3+, Ga@GQDs + Fe3+ and Al@GQDs + Fe3+ are all improved (Fig. 3 b). The relative fluorescence ratios increase from 0.36 to 0.86, 0.76 and 0.56 (Fig. 3 c), respectively, confirming the universality of this strategy. As comparison, most heavy metal ions can quench fluorescence, while non-transition metal ions can maintain even enhance the fluorescence emission of GQDs.

Here, we take Fe3+ as example to illustrate the possible PL quenching and PL maintenance mechanism of GQDs. Fig. 4 shows the C1s XPS spectra of Sc@GQDs (Fig. 4 a), GQDs + Fe3+ (Fig. 4 b) and Sc@GQDs + Fe3+ (Fig. 4 c), which can be decomposed into four peaks, corresponding to different functional groups, i.e., C-C/C=C at 284.70 eV, C-O at 286.13 eV, C=O at 287.28 eV and O-C=O at 288.50 eV. The contents of C-C/C=C in Sc@GQDs, GQDs + Fe3+ and Sc@GQDs + Fe3+ are 62.58%, 49.38%, and 61.40%, respectively. Clearly, the contents of C-C/C=C in Sc@GQDs and Sc@GQDs + Fe3+are very close with each other, while that in GQDs + Fe3+ is significantly lower, indicating that the combination of C-C/C=C groups and Fe3+ leads to the decrease of C-C/C=C groups. Due to the cation-π interaction, cations will affect the conjugated double bond of aromatic groups in GQDs. Therefore, we can speculate that the PL quenching may be ascribed to the reduction of π-conjugated regions, which dominate the PL of GQDs. However, the content of C-C/C=C groups in Sc@GQDs + Fe3+ does not decrease, implying that Fe3+ will not be adsorbed on the π-conjugated regions. On the other hand, the contents of C-O in Sc@GQDs, GQDs + Fe3+ and Sc@GQDs + Fe3+ are 25.17%, 34.16% and 22.57%. On the contrary, the contents of C-O in GQDs + Fe3+ are much higher than those in Sc@GQDs and Sc@GQDs + Fe3+, indicating that Sc3+ ions are bound to the C-O groups, which is in line with FT-IR spectra (Fig. 1 d). Furthermore, Sc3+ ions at these binding sites do no harm to the PL of GQDs, and then Sc3+ adsorbed on the oxygen-containing groups can prevent Fe3+ ions from being adsorbed on the π-conjugated regions of GQDs. Thus, Sc@GQDs possess significant PL stability in heavy metal solution of Fe3+.

The PL intensity of fluorescent powder prepared from GQDs and starch is studied. GQDs aqueous solution was simply mixed with starch, then water was evaporated and the solution was dried into GQDs powder. GQDs + Fe3+, Sc@GQDs and Sc@GQDs + Fe3+ powder was prepared by the same method. As shown in Fig. 5 , the as-prepared powder is pale yellow under natural light, close to the color of GQDs solution. GQDs, Sc@GQDs and Sc@GQDs + Fe3+ powder shows dazzling blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light, while GQDs + Fe3+ does not emit fluorescence, indicating that the PL properties of GQDs, Sc@GQDs, Sc@GQDs + Fe3+ remain stable after the blending and drying process. However, Fe3+ can still quench the fluorescence of pristine GQDs even in powder state. We further observe the influence of time on the PL intensity of various powder. As shown in Fig. 5 (b-d), the PL intensity of GQDs powder begins to decay at day 40, while that of Sc@GQDs and Sc@GQDs + Fe3+ powder remain almost unchanged. The results further confirm that the addition of Sc3+ has a positive effect on the maintenance of PL intensity of GQDs.

Since Sc@GQDs own better PL stability than pristine GQDs, we attempt to explore their potential applications in the fluorescent flexible device and fluorescent ink. Here, we mixed Sc@GQDs with PVA to make composite film. Under the 365 nm UV light, the pristine PVA film (left in Fig. 6
a-b) exhibits almost no fluorescence, while the Sc@GQDs/PVA film (right in Fig. 6 a-b) emits fluorescence brightly. Compared with the pristine PVA film, Sc@GQDs/PVA film retains the PL property. In addition, PVA film has good flexibility and can be widely used in flexible devices [37,38]. The left of Fig. 6 c-d shows the good flexibility of the PVA film, and there is no crack on the film surface even after multiple bending. Similarly, Sc@GQDs /PVA film has no obvious crack after repeated bending (right in Fig. 6 c-d), which inherits the good flexibility of PVA film. Thus, it can be seen that Sc@GQDs/PVA film has excellent flexibility and PL properties. The Sc@GQDs/PVA film can be prepared into arbitrary shape devices, which has great prospect in the application of fluorescent flexible device. Eventually, we use Sc@GQDs to prepare a kind of fluorescent ink [7,39]. A mixed cellulose filter membrane (MCF) was prepared and the MCF membranes were adopted as paper to write and draw using homemade ink with Sc@GQDs. We observe that the words and pattern are blurred under natural light (Fig. 6 e and 6g) and clearly visible under 365 nm UV light (Fig. 6 f and 6h), which validates that Sc@GQDs has great application potential in the fields of fluorescent ink or anti-counterfeiting ink. Under the states of solid powder, composite film and fluorescent ink, the PL stability of Sc@GQDs has also been further confirmed. We therefore propose a superior PL maintenance strategy that GQDs are modified with Sc3+, which shows great potential in the fluorescent materials.

In summary, we have developed a PL maintenance strategy that GQDs are modified by Sc3+ (Sc@GQDs), using one-pot hydrothermal route. The prepared Sc@GQDs own better fluorescent properties than pristine GQDs, as Sc@GQDs can maintain PL in heavy metal ion environment. The mechanism is ascribed to the interactions between Sc3+ and GQDs and the presence of Sc3+ that prevent GQDs from π-π stacking. On the other hand, Sc3+ ions are adsorbed on the edge of functional groups of GQDs based on UV-vis and FT-IR spectra. And these binding sites do no harm to the PL property of GQDs. This strategy possesses the universality for other metal ions, such as Ga3+ and Al3+. Furthermore, GQDs prepared by this strategy show great potential in the applications of fluorescent flexible device and fluorescent ink.

Juan Yang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Pei Li: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Visualization. Zailing Song: Formal analysis, Visualization. Jie Li: Formal analysis, Visualization. Huang Yang: Formal analysis, Visualization. Fan Yan: Formal analysis, Visualization. Lu Li: Formal analysis, Visualization. Can Xu: Formal analysis, Visualization. Junlang Chen: Resource, Supervision, Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Liang Chen: Resource, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (12074341, U1832150, 11875236), the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (No. LQ20B030002), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Provincial Universities of Zhejiang (2020TD001). The authors thank Zhongteng Wang and Prof. Jing Xu for their helpful discussion.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Glowing graphene quantum dots and carbon dots: properties, syntheses, and biological applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. T. Zheng, A. Ananthanarayanan, K. Q. Luo, and P. Chen, Glowing Graphene Quantum Dots and Carbon Dots: Properties, Syntheses, and Biological Applications, Small. 11 (2015) 1620–1636. https://doi.org/10.1002/smll.201402648.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Surface chemistry routes to modulate the photoluminescence of graphene quantum dots: from fluorescence mechanism to up-conversion bioimaging applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Zhu, J. Zhang, S. Tang, C. Qiao, L. Wang, H. Wang, X. Liu, B. Li, Y. Li, W. Yu, X. Wang, H. Sun, and B. Yang, Surface Chemistry Routes to Modulate the Photoluminescence of Graphene Quantum Dots: From Fluorescence Mechanism to Up-Conversion Bioimaging Applications, Adv. Funct. Mater. 22(2012)4732–4740. https://doi.org/10.1002/adfm.201201499.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The photoluminescence mechanism in carbon dots (graphene quantum dots, carbon nanodots, and polymer dots): current state and future perspective

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Zhu, Y. Song, X. Zhao, J. Shao, J. Zhang, and B. Yang, The photoluminescence mechanism in carbon dots (graphene quantum dots, carbon nanodots, and polymer dots): current state and future perspective, Nano Res. 8(2015)355–381 https://doi.org/10.1007/s12274-014-0644-3.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Amphiphilic graphene quantum dots as self-targeted fluorescence probes for cell nucleus imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Wang, B. Wu, W. Li, S. Wang, Z. Li, M. Li, D. Pan, M. Wu, Amphiphilic Graphene Quantum Dots as Self-Targeted Fluorescence Probes for Cell Nucleus Imaging, Adv. Biosyst. 2 (2018) 1700191. https://doi.org/10.1002/adbi.201700191.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Exceptionally high payload of the ir780 iodide on folic acid-functionalized graphene quantum dots for targeted photothermal therapy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Li, S. Zhou, Y. Li, X. Li, J. Zhu, L. Fan, S. Yang, Exceptionally High Payload of the IR780 Iodide on Folic Acid-Functionalized Graphene Quantum Dots for Targeted Photothermal Therapy, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces. 9 (2017) 22332–22341. https://doi.org/10.1021/acsami.7b07267.
